injury__illness_details.describe_part_of_body_affected: Lower back (lumbar region) with pain radiating slightly into the left hip and upper left thigh; minor muscle tightness also noted in upper back/shoulder area from compensating posture later in the day.
injury__illness_details.department_school_location: Public Works Department – Solid Waste Division (Central Landfill, Fairbanks, AK)
injury__illness_details.describe_nature_of_injury_illness: Acute lower back strain/sprain likely involving soft tissue and musculature in the lumbar area, with associated muscle spasm and stiffness. No apparent fracture or dislocation; primary symptoms are sharp pain with bending and lifting, and dull ache when standing or sitting for extended periods.
injury__illness_details.describe_how_the_injury_illness_happened:
On Tuesday, 2025-12-09, I was working my regular day shift at the Fairbanks North Star Borough Central Landfill as a Solid Waste Equipment Operator within the Public Works – Solid Waste Division. My shift started at 07:00. Throughout the morning I had been performing routine tasks, including directing customers at the tipping floor, operating a loader intermittently, and manually moving some smaller items that had fallen outside of the designated drop area.

At approximately 14:30–14:35, I was helping reposition a partially filled 96-gallon rolling garbage cart that had become wedged at an angle between a concrete curb and a metal post near the public drop-off area. The cart was heavier than a standard household cart because it contained compacted mixed waste, including several bags of household trash, some small scrap metal pieces, and what felt like a dense bag of construction debris. I estimate the cart weighed between 150–200 pounds based on my experience moving similar containers.

I initially tried to reposition the cart by standing to the left side of it, grabbing the handle with my right hand and the upper rim with my left hand, and pulling it backwards to free the wheels from the gap. Because the cart was stuck against the post and curb, I had to twist my upper body to the left while my feet stayed mostly planted facing forward on slightly uneven, partially frozen gravel. As I pulled and twisted at the same time, I felt a sudden sharp twinge and then a more intense pulling sensation in the middle-to-lower part of my back, slightly left of my spine. It felt like something “grabbed” or “locked up” in my lower back.

I immediately stopped what I was doing and stood still for a few seconds. The pain was initially around a 7 out of 10 when I tried to straighten up fully. I was able to stand, but I noticed it was painful to bend forward or twist. I did not fall, and nothing struck me directly; the injury was caused by the awkward lifting and twisting motion while exerting force on the heavy cart.

Weather and surface conditions may have contributed: it was cold (around 10–15°F), and the ground surface in that area was a mix of compacted gravel with some icy and wet patches from melted snow and vehicle traffic. Because I was trying to avoid slipping, I kept my feet planted more rigidly than usual, which likely increased the twisting motion through my lower back instead of allowing my feet to move naturally.

After feeling the pain, I carefully set the cart back down and stepped away. I told my coworker, Daniel Ortiz, who was about 15–20 feet away directing traffic, that I had just hurt my back. He came over and asked if I was okay. I told him I had pulled something in my lower back while trying to free the cart. Daniel suggested I notify our supervisor.

I then contacted my supervisor, Michael R. Jensen, by radio and let him know I had injured my back while moving a stuck garbage cart at the public drop-off area. He came over within a few minutes. By the time he arrived, the pain had become more constant and was radiating slightly into my left hip area. He asked me to describe what happened, and I explained the twisting and pulling motion and that I felt a sudden sharp pain followed by ongoing soreness and tightness. He advised me to stop doing any further lifting for the rest of the shift and to report the incident to Risk Management.

For the remainder of the shift, I tried to perform only light tasks such as directing customers and doing minimal walking, but even that caused increasing stiffness. When I sat down for a short break around 15:15 and then stood up again, my lower back felt significantly tighter, and it was more difficult to straighten up fully without pain. At that point I decided I should seek medical evaluation to make sure I had not done more serious damage.

There were no prior incidents earlier that day that caused pain; this was a single, specific event associated with the awkward, forceful maneuver while the cart was stuck. I was wearing my standard PPE (high-visibility vest, work gloves, safety boots, and winter gear) and following usual procedures, but I underestimated how much I should have either asked for help sooner or repositioned myself to avoid twisting while pulling. In hindsight, I should have either used mechanical assistance (e.g., the loader) or asked a coworker to help lift and pivot the cart together to reduce strain on my back.

To summarize, the injury occurred on the employer’s premises at the Central Landfill while I was on duty, performing my regular work tasks, and was caused by lifting and twisting my torso while pulling a heavy, stuck rolling cart on uneven, icy ground. No other employees or customers were directly involved in the physical motion that led to the injury, and there was no assault or intentional act by any person.

initial_medical_treatment.medical_facility_name: Tanana Valley Clinic – Occupational Health Department
